does: This paper addresses story rewriting by proposing a preference-aligned approach that goes beyond surface-level style transfer. The authors introduce StoryLensBench, a large-scale benchmark containing structured stories, multi-dimensional reader preference profiles, and ranked rewritten narrative samples. They develop StoryLensEval, a reward model for predicting reader satisfaction with rewritten stories, and StoryLensWriter, a two-stage model combining supervised fine-tuning with GRPO-based reinforcement learning for narrative generation. Their human studies demonstrate that context-aware narrative enrichment yields 24.5% improvement in preference alignment compared to style-transfer-only baselines that achieve only 2.3% gains, establishing comprehensive evaluation metrics for fidelity, coherence, and reader satisfaction.
connects: This work extends prior research on neural text generation and style transfer by recognizing that story rewriting requires deeper narrative adaptation than existing methods provide. It builds on reward modeling approaches used in preference-aligned language model training while applying them to the creative domain of narrative generation. The paper engages with long-form text generation challenges and the broader problem of aligning generated content with diverse human preferences, combining insights from story understanding, narrative coherence modeling, and reinforcement learning from human feedback that have emerged across recent NLP and generation literature.
enables: This benchmark and methodology enable future research in preference-driven creative writing systems that respect user preferences while maintaining narrative consistency. The StoryLensEval reward model could serve as a reusable component for other narrative generation tasks, and the two-stage training approach combining supervised learning with preference-based reinforcement learning offers a template for other creative text domains. The framework opens pathways toward more personalized story recommendation and adaptation systems, interactive narrative generation tools that respond to reader feedback, and deeper investigation into what makes narratives satisfying across different reader populations and preference dimensions.
